questions.question: The Spanish Civil War (1936–1939) was a brutal conflict that served as a prelude to World War II. Which two sides fought, and which foreign powers supported each?
options: Royalists vs. Republicans — Britain backed the Royalists, France backed the Republicans
Nationalists vs. Republicans — Nazi Germany and Fascist Italy backed the Nationalists; the Soviet Union backed the Republicans
Fascists vs. Communists — the US backed the Fascists, Britain backed the Communists
Republicans vs. Monarchists — the Vatican backed the Monarchists, the US backed the Republicans
questions.answer: Nationalists vs. Republicans — Nazi Germany and Fascist Italy backed the Nationalists; the Soviet Union backed the Republicans
questions.explanation: General Francisco Franco's Nationalists, backed by Hitler and Mussolini with troops, weapons, and aircraft, fought the elected Republican government, backed by Soviet arms and the International Brigades (volunteers from around the world, including Ernest Hemingway and George Orwell). Franco won in 1939 and ruled Spain as dictator until 1975. The war served as a testing ground for German and Italian military tactics later used in WWII.
questions.memory_hook: Spain's civil war (1936–39) was a proxy battle — Hitler and Mussolini backed Franco's Nationalists; the Soviets backed the Republicans; Hemingway and Orwell fought alongside the Republic.

questions.question: Ferdinand Magellan's expedition (1519–1522) completed the first circumnavigation of the globe. Did Magellan himself complete the journey?
options: Yes, he captained the entire voyage and returned to Spain
No, he was killed in the Philippines in 1521; only 18 survivors under Juan Sebastián Elcano returned
No, he turned back halfway due to illness and died in Portugal
Yes, though he arrived months after the main fleet
questions.answer: No, he was killed in the Philippines in 1521; only 18 survivors under Juan Sebastián Elcano returned
questions.explanation: Magellan was killed in the Battle of Mactan in the Philippines on April 27, 1521, after intervening in a local conflict. The expedition's surviving ship, Victoria, under Juan Sebastián Elcano, completed the circumnavigation — returning to Spain in September 1522 with only 18 of the original 270 crew. The voyage proved Earth was round and far larger than Columbus had estimated.
questions.memory_hook: Magellan launched the first circumnavigation but never finished it — he was killed in the Philippines in 1521; only 18 of 270 crew made it back to Spain.

questions.question: In a 1946 speech in Fulton, Missouri, Winston Churchill coined a famous Cold War phrase to describe the division of Europe. What was it?
options: The Iron Fist
The Iron Curtain
The Steel Wall
The Great Divide
questions.explanation: Churchill's 'Sinews of Peace' speech on March 5, 1946, declared: 'From Stettin in the Baltic to Trieste in the Adriatic, an iron curtain has descended across the Continent.' Though Churchill was no longer Prime Minister, the speech crystallized Western anxieties about Soviet expansion and is widely seen as a defining moment marking the start of the Cold War.
questions.memory_hook: Churchill's 1946 Fulton speech coined 'Iron Curtain' — from Stettin to Trieste, the phrase immediately defined the Cold War divide between East and West.

questions.question: On May 6, 1954, Roger Bannister achieved what was considered humanly impossible in athletics. What did he do?
options: Ran 100 meters in under 10 seconds for the first time
Ran a mile in under 4 minutes for the first time
High jumped over 7 feet for the first time
Completed a marathon in under 2 hours for the first time
questions.answer: Ran a mile in under 4 minutes for the first time
questions.explanation: Oxford medical student Roger Bannister ran the mile in 3 minutes 59.4 seconds at Oxford's Iffley Road track on May 6, 1954, becoming the first human to break the 4-minute barrier. The record stood for just 46 days before Australian John Landy broke it. Today the mile record stands at 3:43.13 (Hicham El Guerrouj, 1999). Bannister later had a distinguished career as a neurologist.
questions.memory_hook: Bannister ran the first sub-4-minute mile in 1954 (3:59.4) — the record fell 46 days later, proving the barrier was psychological as much as physical.

questions.question: German driver Michael Schumacher dominated Formula 1 in the early 2000s. How many World Drivers' Championships did he win in his career?
questions.explanation: Schumacher won 7 World Drivers' Championships — 2 with Benetton (1994, 1995) and 5 consecutive with Ferrari (2000–2004). His record of 7 titles stood until Lewis Hamilton matched it in 2020. Schumacher also set records for most race wins and pole positions, many of which have since been broken by Hamilton. A skiing accident in 2013 left him with serious brain injuries.
questions.memory_hook: Schumacher's 7 F1 championships (2 with Benetton, 5 straight with Ferrari 2000–04) stood as the record until Lewis Hamilton matched him in 2020.

questions.question: Michel Gondry's 2004 film Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind follows a couple who each have their memories of the other erased. Who wrote the screenplay?
options: Michel Gondry
Charlie Kaufman
Spike Jonze
David O. Russell
questions.explanation: Charlie Kaufman wrote the screenplay — his fourth film as a writer to explore themes of memory, identity, and consciousness (following Being John Malkovich, Human Nature, and Adaptation). The title comes from Alexander Pope's poem 'Eloisa to Abelard.' Jim Carrey and Kate Winslet star, with Carrey giving one of his most restrained dramatic performances.
questions.memory_hook: Charlie Kaufman's Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind — its title from an Alexander Pope poem — asks whether we'd choose to forget a painful love if we could.

questions.question: Martin Scorsese's 2006 crime thriller The Departed won Best Picture at the Academy Awards. It was a remake of which 2002 Hong Kong film?
options: Infernal Affairs
Hard Boiled
Election
PTU
questions.explanation: The Departed remakes the Hong Kong film Infernal Affairs (2002) by Andrew Lau and Alan Mak, which starred Tony Leung and Andy Lau as a cop infiltrating the mob and a mob mole infiltrating the police simultaneously. Scorsese's version transplanted the story to Boston, with Leonardo DiCaprio, Matt Damon, and Jack Nicholson. It was Scorsese's long-overdue first Oscar for Best Director.
questions.memory_hook: The Departed (2006) remakes Hong Kong's Infernal Affairs — Scorsese finally won his first Best Director Oscar adapting a story about a cop and a criminal each infiltrating the other's world.

questions.question: Paul Thomas Anderson's 2007 film There Will Be Blood is centered on a ruthless oil prospector in early 20th-century California. On what novel is it loosely based?
options: Upton Sinclair's Oil!
John Steinbeck's East of Eden
Frank Norris's McTeague
Jack London's Burning Daylight
questions.explanation: Anderson loosely adapted Upton Sinclair's 1927 muckraking novel Oil!, shifting the focus from the son to the father figure — Daniel Plainview, played by Daniel Day-Lewis in an Oscar-winning performance. The film's final scene and the line 'I'm finished!' became instantly iconic. Jonny Greenwood's dissonant score, which was controversially ruled ineligible for an Oscar, is considered one of the best film scores of the 2000s.
questions.memory_hook: There Will Be Blood loosely adapts Upton Sinclair's Oil! — Daniel Day-Lewis's 'I drink your milkshake!' and 'I'm finished!' became two of cinema's most quoted lines.

questions.question: The Ural Mountains run roughly north-south through Russia and are considered a natural boundary. What do they traditionally divide?
options: Northern Russia from Southern Russia along the 60th parallel
European Russia from Asian Russia (Siberia)
Russia from Kazakhstan and China
The taiga forest zone from the steppe grasslands
questions.explanation: The Urals stretch about 2,500 km from the Arctic coast to the Kazakh steppe, forming the conventional geographic boundary between Europe and Asia. They are relatively low in elevation (highest peak: Mount Narodnaya at 1,895 m) and have been historically permeable — Mongol armies crossed them in the 13th century, and Cossack explorers crossed them moving east in the 16th century.
questions.memory_hook: The Ural Mountains are Europe's eastern wall — they're low enough that Mongol armies and Cossack explorers crossed them easily, but they mark the Europe–Asia boundary.

questions.question: Australia's Great Artesian Basin is one of the world's largest underground freshwater sources. What makes it unusual among aquifers?
options: It is entirely saline and cannot be used for drinking water
It is a confined artesian aquifer — water rises to the surface under natural pressure without pumping
It sits beneath a desert and was formed by ancient glaciers
It is slowly expanding, threatening to flood coastal areas of Australia
questions.answer: It is a confined artesian aquifer — water rises to the surface under natural pressure without pumping
questions.explanation: The Great Artesian Basin underlies about 22% of Australia, spanning parts of Queensland, New South Wales, South Australia, and the Northern Territory. Because the aquifer is confined between impermeable rock layers, water that was recharged in Queensland highlands is under enough pressure to flow to the surface through bores without pumping — critical for cattle stations in the arid interior. The water in deep sections is up to 2 million years old.
questions.memory_hook: Australia's Great Artesian Basin covers 22% of the continent — water recharged in Queensland mountains flows under pressure to the arid outback, no pumping required, some of it 2 million years old.

questions.question: Michael Faraday's 1831 discovery of electromagnetic induction became the basis for electric generators. What is the principle of electromagnetic induction?
options: A steady magnetic field creates a constant electric current in nearby conductors
A changing magnetic field induces an electric current in a nearby conductor
An electric current flowing through a wire creates a magnetic field around it
Two magnets spinning near each other generate heat, which is converted to electricity
questions.answer: A changing magnetic field induces an electric current in a nearby conductor
questions.explanation: Faraday discovered that moving a magnet through a coil of wire induced an electric current — the change in magnetic flux causes electrons to flow. This principle underpins virtually all electrical generation: coal, nuclear, and hydroelectric plants all ultimately spin magnets inside coils to produce electricity. James Clerk Maxwell later gave this a mathematical foundation in his equations of electromagnetism.
questions.memory_hook: Faraday's 1831 law: a moving (changing) magnet through a wire coil makes electricity flow — every power plant on Earth still uses this principle to generate electricity.

questions.question: Bats and dolphins both use echolocation to navigate and hunt. How does echolocation work?
options: They detect infrared heat signatures emitted by prey
They emit high-frequency sound pulses and interpret the returning echoes to build a map of their surroundings
They sense disturbances in Earth's magnetic field caused by moving objects
They detect electrical fields generated by the muscles of moving animals
questions.answer: They emit high-frequency sound pulses and interpret the returning echoes to build a map of their surroundings
questions.explanation: Echolocating animals emit ultrasonic calls (bats) or clicks (dolphins) and analyze the returning echoes to determine distance, size, shape, and texture of objects. Bats can locate insects as thin as a human hair in complete darkness; dolphins use a fatty organ called the melon in their forehead to focus sound beams. The term 'echolocation' was coined by naturalist Donald Griffin in 1944.
questions.memory_hook: Echolocation works like biological sonar — bats and dolphins send out sound pulses and read the echoes, letting bats catch hair-thin insects in total darkness.
